title: Beep Panic

jokes.text: This is a defecation-free store. That's the kind of feature you dream about. No defecating! Urinators... welcome. But if you are a defecator, or planning on defecating anytime soon, don't come down here.
jokes.setup: Larry is promoting his new coffee shop's features
jokes.punchline: He treats bathroom policies like premium amenities, with overly formal language about bodily functions
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry David absurdism - treating crude bathroom policies as sophisticated business features. The formal tone contrasting with 'defecating' is brilliant character work.

jokes.text: (sign shatters)
jokes.setup: Larry boasting about how impressive his coffee shop is
jokes.punchline: The defecation-free sign immediately breaks, undermining his sales pitch
jokes.explanation: Classic visual punctuation - the universe immediately undermining Larry's confidence. Perfect timing and economy.

jokes.text: Larry, that is the most amazing urinary experience I've ever had in my life.
jokes.setup: Customer giving feedback on Larry's coffee shop
jokes.punchline: Treating urination like fine dining with overly enthusiastic praise
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the bathroom policy, treating mundane bodily functions with restaurant review language.

jokes.text: Bean-meister.
jokes.setup: Larry needs to give Leon a job title
jokes.punchline: Creating a ridiculous Germanic compound word for coffee shop employee
jokes.explanation: Solid wordplay combining 'bean' with the German '-meister' suffix. Economical and fits the characters.

jokes.text: I'm banned. And my mother. Do you believe it? Who's a saint, by the way.
jokes.setup: Mocha Joe can't go spy on Larry's coffee shop
jokes.punchline: Not only is he banned, but his saintly mother too - absurd overreach by Larry
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing how petty Larry can be, banning even innocent family members. The 'saint' qualifier adds nice character detail.

jokes.text: In case there's any handshaking to be done, I know I'm safe. Have a little squirt.
jokes.setup: Larry greeting Ted Danson at his coffee shop
jokes.punchline: Larry obsessively using hand sanitizer before potential contact, treating handshaking like contamination risk
jokes.explanation: Pure Larry neurosis - preemptively sanitizing for social interaction. 'Have a little squirt' is wonderfully awkward phrasing.

jokes.text: I'm going out with Cheryl tonight. Thought you might want to know.
jokes.setup: Ted making casual conversation with Larry
jokes.punchline: Ted awkwardly announcing he's dating Larry's ex-wife to make Larry uncomfortable
jokes.explanation: Solid cringe moment - Ted's passive-aggressive oversharing designed to needle Larry. Economical setup and payoff.

jokes.text: Son of a bitch! He's got the beans.
jokes.setup: Mocha Joe tasting Larry's superior coffee
jokes.punchline: His defeated realization that Larry has genuinely good coffee, not just spite
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing Mocha Joe's grudging respect. 'He's got the beans' is a nice coffee shop twist on admitting defeat.

jokes.text: Whoa, whoa. Get your own.
jokes.setup: Someone trying to taste Ted's coffee again
jokes.punchline: Ted's sudden possessiveness over coffee after initially being generous
jokes.explanation: Shows how quickly friendliness can turn to selfishness over good coffee

jokes.text: I had, like, this total beep panic. 'Cause you just want to get away from, from the other person. It's humiliating.
jokes.setup: Larry explaining why he threw his phone and fled after getting caught texting
jokes.punchline: Creating a specific psychological condition 'beep panic' for universal embarrassing experience
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Larry observation - coining 'beep panic' for that specific modern embarrassment. Perfect character voice and universal relatability.

jokes.text: Maybe the car goes through the light and hits a stroller. Who's responsible? So I killed a baby? All because you were texting. And now you killed a child.
jokes.setup: Freddy imagining worst-case scenario from Larry's beep panic
jokes.punchline: Escalating from texting mishap to baby murder with Larry's horrified reactions
jokes.explanation: Dark escalation comedy - taking a minor incident to absurd tragic conclusion. Larry's repeated 'I killed a baby?' is great reaction work.

jokes.text: Or you can go this way. This baby was the Hitler of the future.
jokes.setup: Freddy trying to console Larry about hypothetically killing a baby
jokes.punchline: Suggesting the baby would have grown up to be Hitler, so it's actually good
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark twist on the escalation - solving moral dilemma with time travel Hitler logic. Perfectly economical and absurd.

jokes.text: Listen to me. I love the imagination, but it was a real story with real people. It didn't need sci-fi.
jokes.setup: Freddy adding increasingly absurd hypothetical elements to Larry's story
jokes.punchline: Larry treating baby-Hitler as 'sci-fi' that's cluttering his simple anecdote
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry response - treating outrageous hypotheticals as unwanted narrative embellishment. Great character consistency and meta-humor about storytelling.

jokes.text: Even though the baby's dead, I still wanna eat.
jokes.setup: Larry has been discussing hypothetically killing a baby
jokes.punchline: Casually transitioning back to mundane concerns while referencing the dark scenario
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark transition - treating baby murder as minor inconvenience to lunch plans. Perfect economy and character voice.

jokes.text: They feel that unless they can play a mentally challenged person... that they're not, uh, worthy as an actor.
jokes.setup: Jeff explaining why Lewis is doing Flowers for Algernon
jokes.punchline: Revealing the pretentious Hollywood actor trope of disability roles as prestige projects
jokes.explanation: Good Hollywood satire - exposing the cynical calculation behind 'serious' acting choices. Well-observed industry critique.

jokes.text: I have diarrhea, so... I'm gonna come right back in a little bit and check on you, okay?
jokes.setup: Waitress apologizing for slow service
jokes.punchline: Casually announcing she has diarrhea to explain the delay
jokes.explanation: Peak cringe comedy - the ultimate TMI customer service moment that sets up future callbacks

jokes.text: I have diarrhea, so... I'm gonna come right back in a little bit and check on you, okay?
jokes.setup: Waitress apologizing for delay in service
jokes.punchline: Oversharing extremely personal medical information as casual explanation
jokes.explanation: Classic cringe comedy - inappropriate oversharing in service context. Sets up character trait that will pay off later.

jokes.text: Having or had?
jokes.setup: Waitress announcing she has diarrhea
jokes.punchline: Larry asking for grammatical clarification on her bathroom emergency
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry response - focusing on linguistic precision during medical oversharing. Brilliant character work showing his social priorities.

jokes.text: I pretend there's something wrong with my car, and then I grab a ton of it.
jokes.setup: Larry explaining how he gets licorice from BMW dealership
jokes.punchline: Admitting to elaborate deception for free candy
jokes.explanation: Great character reveal - Larry's petty scheming for minor rewards. Shows his willingness to lie for trivial gains.

jokes.text: The air comes out and makes a bit of a whistling sound. (whistling) The car is whistling.
jokes.setup: Larry making up car problems to justify dealership visit
jokes.punchline: His elaborate sound effects demonstration for fake whistling car
jokes.explanation: Good callback to his deception scheme. The physical whistling demonstration adds nice absurd detail to his con.

jokes.text: I will find out where the licorice comes from. And hopefully we will figure out what the problem is with your car, too.
jokes.setup: Salesman dealing with Larry's obviously fake car problems
jokes.punchline: Treating the licorice inquiry as equally legitimate business, showing he knows Larry's game
jokes.explanation: Nice subtle acknowledgment that the salesman sees through Larry's scheme but plays along professionally.

jokes.text: You can't-- $1.30 a cup? We're not gonna make any money doing that. Put in some coat racks! No way! It's Southern California! One coat rack! We don't need a coat rack!
jokes.setup: Mocha Joe's coffee shop struggling to compete with Larry
jokes.punchline: Absurd argument about coat racks as business solution in warm climate
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Joe's desperation. The California climate logic makes the coat rack suggestion even more absurd.

jokes.text: Hey, you remember me? Ted Danson? Cheers? Becker? Cheers? Any-- You remember me?
jokes.setup: Ted trying to get recognition from customers
jokes.punchline: Desperately listing his credits to uninterested people, repeating 'Cheers' twice
jokes.explanation: Good celebrity ego cringe - Ted's need for recognition leading to awkward self-promotion. The repeated 'Cheers' shows his desperation.

jokes.text: they're DVDs of all the latest movies the Academy gives us so we can vote. The thing is, though, you gotta give 'em back to me, 'cause they're all watermarked or something. I could get into trouble.
jokes.setup: Ted offering to lend Joe screener DVDs to help him relax
jokes.punchline: Casually explaining the illegal nature while setting up future plot complications
jokes.explanation: Good setup for later payoff. Ted's casual mention of getting in trouble foreshadows the FBI plot while showing his careless generosity.

jokes.text: From here? No, I'm good.
jokes.setup: Mocha Joe offering Ted coffee from his own shop after Ted helped him
jokes.punchline: Ted politely declining coffee from the shop he's supposedly supporting
jokes.explanation: Perfect understated reveal that even Ted knows Joe's coffee is inferior. Great economy - says everything in four words.

jokes.text: ♪ Hanukkah, Oh Hanukkah ♪ (enthusiastic singing and dancing)
jokes.setup: Jeff thinking he's going on a golf trip without Susie
jokes.punchline: His over-the-top joyful singing and dancing about the holiday
jokes.explanation: Jeff's uncontainable happiness about time away from Susie expressed through random holiday song

jokes.text: What the fuck are you so happy about right now? Nothing. Bags back in the car. I'm not going.
jokes.setup: Jeff singing happily while Susie observes
jokes.punchline: Susie immediately recognizing and punishing Jeff's obvious happiness about her leaving
jokes.explanation: Perfect relationship dynamics - Susie's immediate detection of Jeff's poorly concealed joy. Great escalation from singing to trip cancellation.

jokes.text: Hold on a second. I gotta make a call. Be quiet. I gotta call Larry David.
jokes.setup: Joe watching Ted Danson screener with his mother
jokes.punchline: Joe interrupting family time to call his business rival
jokes.explanation: Shows Joe's business obsession interrupting even pleasant family moments

jokes.text: I was too happy at the airport drop-off. I was, uh, bouncy. I was dancing.
jokes.setup: Jeff explaining why Susie canceled her trip
jokes.punchline: His honest admission of being too obviously excited about her departure
jokes.explanation: Great character moment - Jeff's self-aware admission of his marital mistake. The physical description makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Of course you're happy. Every husband is thrilled... to drop their wife off at the airport. But you gotta contain it, and you celebrate in the car on the way home!
jokes.setup: Larry commenting on Jeff's airport behavior
jokes.punchline: Stating universal husband truth while explaining proper celebration protocol
jokes.explanation: Excellent observational comedy - Larry laying out the unspoken rules of marriage with perfect logic and timing.

jokes.text: (descending whistle) (plops)
jokes.setup: Diane serving soup to the table
jokes.punchline: Her sweat audibly dropping into the soup with sound effects
jokes.explanation: Perfect gross-out visual gag with sound effects. Sets up major plot point and character conflict. The whistle sound makes it cinematically funny.

jokes.text: Top five most revolting thing I've ever seen in my entire life.
jokes.setup: Larry witnessing the sweat-in-soup incident
jokes.punchline: His dramatic overstatement ranking it among life's worst moments
jokes.explanation: Good Larry overreaction - treating minor grossness as life-defining trauma. Perfect character voice and proportional response.

jokes.text: You unzip again, two weeks, no golf.
jokes.setup: Mr. Takahashi enforcing dress code on Larry
jokes.punchline: Treating jacket zipper as serious disciplinary issue with specific punishment
jokes.explanation: Great character consistency for Takahashi - extreme formality about minor rules. Perfect economy and threatening absurdity.

jokes.text: Everyone knows about diarrhea.
jokes.setup: Larry asking how Takahashi knows about the waitress's condition
jokes.punchline: Matter-of-fact statement that diarrhea is common knowledge
jokes.explanation: Brilliant deadpan delivery making diarrhea sound like public information. Perfect Takahashi character voice and absurd logic.

jokes.text: Well, he fired me, so...
jokes.setup: Larry saying not to take Takahashi's yelling personally
jokes.punchline: Diane's casual revelation that she was actually fired
jokes.explanation: The understated delivery of major news shows Diane's resignation to her situation

jokes.text: You would eat my sweat if I dripped sweat in your soup? I don't know, but to be polite, it's like, I don't know.
jokes.setup: Diane defending her sweating in the soup
jokes.punchline: Larry's disgusted but considerate response about eating sweat for politeness
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the sweat debate. Larry's conflict between disgust and manners is very character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Maybe that's why you don't have a girlfriend or a wife.
jokes.setup: Larry refusing to eat sweat soup
jokes.punchline: Diane suggesting his relationship failures stem from sweat-eating standards
jokes.explanation: Sharp comeback linking Larry's pickiness to his romantic failures. Good character insight delivered with economy.

jokes.text: No one's down with diarrhea, Diane!
jokes.setup: Diane thinking people would understand her diarrhea announcement
jokes.punchline: Larry's exasperated declaration of universal diarrhea intolerance
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry exasperation with great quotable phrasing. 'Down with diarrhea' is brilliantly absurd phrasing for obvious truth.

jokes.text: Sometimes I like a black, and then I'll go weeks with only wanting red. And then I switch back to black.
jokes.setup: Larry asking about licorice preferences
jokes.punchline: Diane's detailed analysis of her seasonal licorice preferences
jokes.explanation: Mild character moment showing Diane's earnest approach to minor preferences

jokes.text: She knows all about cars. She gets Motor Trend magazine. She reads all that stuff.
jokes.setup: Larry bringing Diane to help pick out a car
jokes.punchline: His ridiculous justification for her automotive expertise
jokes.explanation: Larry's elaborate fiction to justify bringing Diane as his 'car expert'

jokes.text: Look, you don't fool me, Mr. David. You're not here to buy a car.
jokes.setup: Larry at BMW dealership with Diane looking at cars
jokes.punchline: Salesman calling out Larry's obvious licorice scam
jokes.explanation: Good setup for escalation. Salesman finally confronting Larry's obvious con game sets up the spite purchase.

jokes.text: I am here to buy a new car. Which one? That one! Yeah. That one!
jokes.setup: Salesman challenging Larry to actually buy a car
jokes.punchline: Larry's impulsive spite purchase of expensive car to prove a point
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry spite behavior - making expensive decision to win argument. Great character consistency and escalation.

jokes.text: A lot of the douchebags at the club have it. No offense.
jokes.setup: Larry admiring his new expensive car
jokes.punchline: Diane casually insulting Larry's social circle while complimenting the car
jokes.explanation: Perfect oblivious insult - Diane doesn't realize she's calling Larry a douchebag

jokes.text: Take it! Take the car. I gave you a car! What's wrong with me?
jokes.setup: Diane saying she has no transportation to work
jokes.punchline: Larry's impulsive generosity immediately followed by self-doubt
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry - grand gesture immediately undermined by his own neurotic self-awareness

jokes.text: I gave you a car! What's wrong with me?
jokes.setup: Larry giving his new BMW to Diane
jokes.punchline: His immediate regret and self-awareness of his impulsive generosity
jokes.explanation: Great character moment - Larry's self-aware recognition of his own impulsive mistake. Perfect setup for coming complications.

jokes.text: I looked at you, you were texting. Somebody beeped you, you had beep panic, you threw the phone away. Look, there's your phone right in the front seat.
jokes.setup: Larry and Diane in car accident
jokes.punchline: Larry using his own beep panic theory to diagnose Diane's accident cause
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback to the beep panic theory coming full circle. Perfect structure and character consistency - Larry diagnosing others with his own neuroses.

jokes.text: What do you mean it's your car? You just give away cars and take 'em back? Oprah doesn't just give out cars and then take them back.
jokes.setup: Larry trying to take back the car he gave Diane after she crashed
jokes.punchline: Diane comparing Larry unfavorably to Oprah's famous car giveaway
jokes.explanation: Brilliant pop culture reference showing the absurdity of Larry's take-back. Diane using Oprah as the moral standard for gift-giving.

jokes.text: When you tell a motherfucker that story, you should say, 'Lo and behold.' When a motherfucker says, 'Lo and behold,' it makes me fuckin' react.
jokes.setup: Larry telling Leon about his car situation
jokes.punchline: Leon's specific advice about dramatic storytelling technique
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leon character voice - turning ordinary story into dramatic performance art. His specific reaction to 'lo and behold' is brilliantly character-specific.

jokes.text: That big motherfucker's the glue.
jokes.setup: Discussing why the golf trip won't work without Jeff
jokes.punchline: Leon's crude but accurate assessment of Jeff's group dynamic role
jokes.explanation: Leon's perfectly concise and profane character assessment of Jeff's importance

jokes.text: Black licorice, black jelly beans, fuckin' blackouts, burnt toast. I don't give a fuck, I eat burnt toast. I take that piece of burnt-ass toast, put it between two pieces of fuckin' pumpernickel, have a burnt-toast pumpernickel sandwich.
jokes.setup: Leon explaining his preference for black licorice
jokes.punchline: Escalating to absurd burnt food combinations in increasingly graphic detail
jokes.explanation: Absolutely brilliant Leon escalation - connecting licorice preference to entire life philosophy about blackened foods. The burnt toast sandwich is perfectly absurd character detail.

jokes.text: (stomach rumbling) (Leon chuckles) (stomach gurgling) Aah! (yells) Oh! (Larry groaning loudly)
jokes.setup: Mocha Joe is trying to have a serious business meeting
jokes.punchline: Both Larry and Leon are struck by severe stomach issues from the licorice
jokes.explanation: Extended physical comedy sequence that pays off the licorice setup. The timing and escalation work well.

jokes.text: (Larry and Leon both experiencing stomach distress simultaneously)
jokes.setup: Both characters ate excessive amounts of German licorice
jokes.punchline: Simultaneous digestive disasters during important meeting with Mocha Joe
jokes.explanation: Great physical comedy payoff to the licorice obsession. Perfect timing during crucial negotiation scene adds to the chaos.

jokes.text: That's negotiating? On what planet is that negotiating? I do it with my mattress guys. I have 'em out there pounding cotton.
jokes.setup: Larry saying Mocha Joe disappeared during their bathroom emergency
jokes.punchline: Leon's comparison to his own business negotiation tactics with mattress salesmen
jokes.explanation: Leon's unique business philosophy - leaving salespeople hanging while making major purchases

jokes.text: I do it with my mattress guys. I have 'em out there pounding cotton. And, uh, family throws out a quote they don't like, I tell 'em to just get out of there, walk away.
jokes.setup: Leon explaining Mocha Joe's negotiation tactic
jokes.punchline: Comparing it to his mattress business strategy with vivid physical description
jokes.explanation: Good Leon business wisdom delivered with characteristic colorful language. 'Pounding cotton' adds nice physical detail to his negotiation theory.

jokes.text: I'm gonna get that operation. You know why? 'Cause I'm smart. I'm smarter than that mouse, that stupid mouse.
jokes.setup: Richard performing Charlie from Flowers for Algernon
jokes.punchline: His intense method acting delivering lines about intelligence while being intellectually disabled character
jokes.explanation: Decent callback to Richard's pretentious acting choice. The irony of the dialogue content adds meta-humor about the role.

jokes.text: Why are you laughing? It's not hilarious. I'm a-- It's dramatic.
jokes.setup: Larry and Freddy laughing at Richard's dramatic performance
jokes.punchline: Richard's offended reaction to their inappropriate laughter at his serious acting
jokes.explanation: Perfect actor ego moment - Richard's wounded pride at their reaction to his 'serious' work

jokes.text: It's like being inside your face. I don't know what my face looks like.
jokes.setup: Larry explaining why car color doesn't matter to him
jokes.punchline: His bizarre analogy about not seeing your own face from the inside
jokes.explanation: Peak Larry logic - his completely unique and absurd but somehow logical analogy

jokes.text: I don't see the hood. By the way, I don't believe you.
jokes.setup: Larry claiming he doesn't know his car's exterior color because he's inside it
jokes.punchline: Susie calling out his obvious lie while Larry doubles down with specific detail
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Larry's absurd theory with Susie's direct challenge. Larry's specific hood detail shows his commitment to the lie.

jokes.text: I see what happened. You were on your cell phone, and somebody yelled, 'Next,' and you panicked, right? Yes. I had 'next panic,' okay?
jokes.setup: Larry getting hit by wheelchair in coffee shop
jokes.punchline: Applying his panic theory to different situation, man admits to 'next panic'
jokes.explanation: Excellent expansion of the beep panic concept to 'next panic' - showing Larry's theory has broader applications. Great structure and callback integration.

jokes.text: I had too much licorice from Bavaria, and it gave me diarrhea, and when I was in the bathroom, he took the screeners!
jokes.setup: FBI investigating Mocha Joe for stolen screeners
jokes.punchline: Joe's elaborate explanation connecting multiple plot threads in desperate defense
jokes.explanation: Brilliant convergence of multiple storylines - licorice, diarrhea, screeners all coming together in Joe's frantic explanation to FBI.

jokes.text: (Richard Lewis experiencing stomach distress during his big dramatic scene)
jokes.setup: Richard performing his serious dramatic role in Flowers for Algernon
jokes.punchline: Licorice-induced digestive problems ruining his prestigious acting moment
jokes.explanation: Perfect culmination - Richard's pretentious acting project destroyed by Larry's licorice gift. Great physical comedy with dramatic irony.

tmdb_overview: Mocha Joe goes to war with his new competitor. Larry takes pity on a waitress and creates friction at the BMW dealership. Richard takes a career-defining role.
